Headline: prompt caching is effectively inert on the primary chat path
This is the single most important finding, and it sits in the just-reworked chat route + anthropic-adapter.ts. Two compounding problems mean the default interview path (anthropic_claude_haiku_4_5_interview_primary) pays full input price on essentially every turn:

1. The system prompt is below the cache floor, so it's never cached. (HIGH)
oracle-system.ts:7 is ~2.6 KB ≈ ~650 tokens. The Anthropic adapter requires ANTHROPIC_HAIKU_CACHE_MIN_TOKENS = 2048 (anthropic-adapter.ts:68, checked in buildSystem at line 312-314). Since stableTokens (~650) < 2048, buildSystem returns a plain string with no cache_control. So even the one thing the architecture caches — the stable system prompt — is never marked cacheable on the default route. (Anthropic genuinely won't cache <2048 tokens on Haiku, so the floor is correct; the fix is to grow the cached stable prefix, not lower the floor — see below.)

2. The chat route puts volatile per-turn content first in the message array, busting conversation prefix-caching. (HIGH)
route.ts:428-436 builds messagesWithContext = [ {volatile dynamicContext}, ...safeMessages ]. dynamicContext (route.ts:194-212) contains the freshly retrieved relevant claims (re-retrieved from the latest message every turn) and open gaps — it changes every single turn. By placing it at position 0, right after the system prompt, the entire conversation history that follows it can never form a stable cacheable prefix across turns. The growing, genuinely-stable conversation history (the ideal cache target) is positioned after the volatile block. The breakpoint logic in anthropic-adapter.ts:360-369 marks the penultimate message, but it's moot — the prefix is already broken at index 1.

This is precisely the failure mode the ContextCompiler exists to prevent (context-compiler.ts:110 assertStableBeforeDynamic) — but the chat route bypasses the compiler's ordering by routing everything through providerOptions.messages and hand-ordering it dynamic-first.

Fix for both: Move the per-turn dynamicContext to the end of the message sequence (append to or place immediately before the latest user turn), so the layout is [system][stable history…][volatile context + latest question]. The conversation prefix then caches incrementally. To get the system prompt over the Haiku floor, fold the semi-stable domain/taxonomy context into the cached stable region so the cached prefix clears 2048 tokens. As-is, the entire cache:{preferLongLivedCache, expectedReuseCount:6,…} block passed at route.ts:450-462 buys nothing on the Anthropic path.

Other AI / caching findings
3. Vertex creates a fresh per-turn explicit cache for multi-turn chat. (MED)
vertex-gemini-adapter.ts:280-329: the non-file prepareExplicitCacheForText caches contents.slice(0,-1) (the whole prior conversation) with a cacheKey that includes prefixText. Every turn the conversation grows → different hash → a brand-new caches.create that's reused once and orphaned next turn. With persistProviderCacheRecord on (route.ts:458), this also churns provider_cached_content rows. Explicit caching only pays off with reuse; for fast back-and-forth chat, implicit caching is cheaper. Gate explicit-cache creation on a stable prefix (system + retrieved context), not the whole conversation, or only for the file-backed PDF path.

4. generateObject drops providerOptions in OpenAI and DeepSeek adapters. (HIGH)
openai-adapter.ts:114 and deepseek-adapter.ts:120 call buildMessages(systemPrompt, userMessage) with no providerOptions, silently discarding any multi-turn messages override and locking temperature at 0.1. Beyond correctness, a truncated single-message prompt won't match the stored multi-turn prefix, defeating DeepSeek/OpenAI prefix caching for structured calls. (Anthropic and Vertex thread it correctly.)

5. Schema-validation failures are swallowed, neutralizing fallback. (MED–HIGH)
All structured adapters do object: (validated ?? parsed) (anthropic-adapter.ts:159, vertex:216, openai/deepseek/qwen). tryZodParse returns null both for "not a Zod schema" and "validation failed" (vertex-gemini-adapter.ts:1015), so a real validation failure is silently swallowed and unvalidated output flows through typed as valid. Because the adapter never throws, ModelRouter.shouldFallback (model-router.ts:149) never sees schema_validation_failure, so the documented schema-repair/fallback flow can't engage.

6. Router fallback uses substring matching on error messages. (MED)
model-router.ts:152-155 matches msg.includes('429' | 'timeout' | …). A validation error whose text contains "timeout", or a model response body containing "429", triggers an unintended provider fallback. Prefer typed status codes (err.status === 429, SDK APIError instances).

7. Qwen cache-token normalization reads an Anthropic-shaped field. (MED)
qwen-adapter.ts normalizeUsage reads prompt_tokens_details.cache_creation_input_tokens — that's the Anthropic shape; DashScope never returns it, so cacheWriteTokens is always empty, and the central usage-normalizer.ts:66 routes Qwen through normalizeOpenAI, which doesn't understand the Responses-API input_tokens_details shape the adapter actually produces. Qwen cache accounting is effectively lost.

8. embedMany ignores OpenAI's index ordering. (HIGH — silent data corruption)
embeddings.ts:82-87 does body.data.map(d => d.embedding) with no sort by index, no length check vs input, no per-vector dim check (unlike embedText). The embeddings API does not guarantee response order matches input order — vectors can be silently attached to the wrong claim/chunk. Sort by d.index, assert data.length === texts.length, and validate each length against EMBEDDING_DIM. Also no request batching (embeddings.ts:71) — a large backfill will 400.

9. Cluster naming uses runText + regex JSON extraction instead of runObject. (MED)
taxonomy-reevaluation.ts nameCluster parses /\{[\s\S]*\}/ out of free text — the exact suboptimal-AI pattern the structured-output layer exists to replace. Use client.runObject with a Zod schema; also set cacheEligible on the stable naming system prompt (each cluster currently re-pays it).

Concurrency / correctness bugs (workers)
10. The per-hour interjection cap is not enforced under concurrency. (HIGH)
teams-live-recall-utterance.ts, lull-interjection.ts, and contradiction-watcher.ts all read rate/cooldown state, then make a multi-second model call, then insert oracleInterventions — with no advisory lock, unique constraint, or re-check. Multiple utterances (and the minute-cron lull task overlapping itself or the other two writers on the same channel) all pass the inLastHour < max gate simultaneously and all post. Gate the read→draft→insert behind pg_try_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(channelId)) with an in-lock re-read, or claim gaps atomically via UPDATE … WHERE status='open' RETURNING.

11. Contradiction pair-dedup query is fragile. (MED–HIGH)
contradiction-watcher.ts uses claimAId IN [a,b] AND claimBId IN [a,b] to test for an existing unordered pair — it can match self-pairs and is loose. Use the explicit (claimAId=a AND claimBId=b) OR (claimAId=b AND claimBId=a).

12. detectionConfidence is hardcoded to the threshold. (MED)
contradiction-watcher.ts passes detectionConfidence: CONTRADICTION_LIVE_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD, so the "confidence ≥ threshold" gate is a tautology and the stored value is meaningless. Either add a numeric confidence to the adjudication schema or drop the field.

13. Document quote→chunk matching silently rejects valid claims. (MED)
document-ingestion.ts picks the evidence chunk via chunk.text.includes(exactQuote); chunks overlap by 150 chars, so a quote spanning a boundary matches no chunk, falls back to insertedChunkIds[0], then fails quote validation against the wrong chunk — the claim is rejected though the quote is genuinely in the doc. Match against full rawText and map the offset back to the covering chunk.

14. Invalid SQL in entity-proposal merge. (HIGH)
packages/oracle-engines/src/extraction/stage-entity-proposal.ts uses ${rawString} = ANY(SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text(...)) — not valid Postgres and a divergence from the repo's endorsed IN (SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text(...)) pattern (per AGENTS.md §"Raw-SQL list parameters"). Likely untested against a real DB.

15. creative_design excluded from operations-systems retrieval, and two domains are uninferable. (MED)
retrieval-plan.ts: inferTopDomainExclusions excludes creative_design (a real domain in domains.ts) for operations queries, silently dropping its claims; meanwhile creative_design and vendor_management have no DOMAIN_KEYWORDS entry, so queries about them fall to global_fallback (full-corpus noise). Add keyword groups or document the intent.

16. Non-transactional multi-write sequences. (MED)
brain-synthesis.ts updates currentVersionId then applies sectionClaims/gaps as separate statements (header claims "transactional" but isn't); taxonomy-reclassification.ts applyProposal handlers move/delete/retire across statements with no transaction and no change-log row on partial failure. Wrap each in db.transaction.

17. claim-extraction.ts orphans messages stuck in 'processing'. (MED)
Messages are flipped to 'processing' ([~line 196]) with no reaper; a crash mid-run leaves them permanently un-extractable (the cron only selects 'pending'). Add a stale-processing reset, mirroring document-ingestion.

Lower-severity / efficiency (condensed)

18	recall.ts	Transient send_chat_message failure re-throws → Trigger retry → dedup skip → interjection silently dropped	MED
19	lull-interjection.ts:184-289	6 sequential queries per channel × sequential channels; hoist global open-gap fetch out of loop, parallelize	MED

What's genuinely well done
The caching architecture is sound: ContextCompiler enforces stable-before-dynamic and computes per-region hashes (context-compiler.ts); cache-utils.ts centralizes TTL/retention policy and static-first flattening; the Vertex explicit-cache lifecycle (Postgres-tracked, cross-process reuse by source_hash, sweeper cleanup) is thorough; the dual retrieval branches share buildPlanMetadataFilters with a CI parity guard. The problem is that the chat route bypasses the compiler's ordering and the default model is below the cache floor, so the well-built machinery doesn't engage where it matters most.

Output:
# The Oracle

The Oracle is an evidence-backed enterprise knowledge graph for POP Creations / Spruce Line. Employees talk to it in chat and upload documents; workers extract operational claims with quote-level evidence; deterministic validators gate promotion; synthesis workers maintain Brain sections; admin screens review the resulting knowledge and AI runs.

Start here:

- `AGENTS.md` — primary developer and AI-session operating guide
- `HANDOFF.md` — active unfinished work, when present
- `docs/architecture.md` — current system design and data flow
- `docs/development.md` — local setup and verification workflow
- `docs/configuration.md` — env vars and runtime config
- `docs/deployment.md` — release/deploy process
- `DECISIONS.md` — architectural decisions and constraints
- `oracle_master_spec.md` — product contract

## Repo map

- `apps/web/` — Next.js 16 web app, admin UI, chat route, Teams transcript webhook (`/api/teams/notifications`), Recall live Teams webhook (`/api/teams/live/recall`)
- `apps/workers/` — Trigger.dev workers (claim extraction, doc ingestion, synthesis, contradiction watcher, Teams transcript ingestion + subscription manager, Recall live Teams utterance processor)
- `packages/ai/` — `OracleAIClient`, retrieval, prompts, provider adapters
- `packages/db/` — Drizzle schema, migrations, seed, DB client
- `packages/oracle-engines/` — deterministic validation/promotion/synthesis logic
- `packages/auth/` — auth linking helpers
- `packages/shared/` — shared types/constants

## Current runtime shape

- Web app: Vercel via `vercel.json`
- Workers: Trigger.dev Cloud via `apps/workers/trigger.config.ts`
- Database/Auth/Storage/Realtime: Supabase Cloud
- AI inference: direct provider adapters only
  - Anthropic via `@anthropic-ai/sdk`
  - Vertex via `@google/genai`
  - OpenAI via `openai`
  - DeepSeek via `openai` against `api.deepseek.com`
  - Qwen via `openai` against DashScope OpenAI-compatible endpoints
- OpenRouter is enrichment-only for the model catalog, not inference

## Fast start

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm typecheck
pnpm --filter @oracle/web build
pnpm dev
```

For a real local setup you also need `.env.local`, Supabase access, and Vertex ADC. Use `docs/development.md` and `docs/configuration.md` for the exact steps.

## Notes

- `HANDOFF.md` should exist only when work is unfinished. If it is missing, there is no active handoff file.
- `AGENTS.md` is the canonical guide for future sessions. Keep it current when behavior or operations change.

payload.message:
My take: the assessment is mostly credible, and the headline is right. I would not treat every item as equally urgent, but the top third identifies real issues in current `main`.

The strongest findings I verified:

- Prompt caching on the main Anthropic chat path is probably underperforming badly. The Oracle system prompt is ~2,838 chars, roughly 710 tokens, while Haiku requires 2,048 estimated stable tokens before `cache_control` is applied in [anthropic-adapter.ts](C:/repos/oracle/packages/ai/src/providers/anthropic-adapter.ts:312). The chat route also prepends volatile runtime context before history in [route.ts](C:/repos/oracle/apps/web/app/api/chat/route.ts:428), which makes conversation-prefix reuse much less useful.
- OpenAI and DeepSeek `generateObject` do drop `providerOptions.messages`: [openai-adapter.ts](C:/repos/oracle/packages/ai/src/providers/openai-adapter.ts:114), [deepseek-adapter.ts](C:/repos/oracle/packages/ai/src/providers/deepseek-adapter.ts:120). That is a real correctness and caching gap.
- Structured validation failures are swallowed. `tryZodParse` returns `null` on failed Zod validation in [vertex-gemini-adapter.ts](C:/repos/oracle/packages/ai/src/providers/vertex-gemini-adapter.ts:1015), and adapters fall back to returning the parsed raw object. That means fallback/repair won’t see schema failures.
- `embedMany` is too trusting: it maps response data directly with no length, dimension, or index validation in [embeddings.ts](C:/repos/oracle/packages/ai/src/embeddings.ts:82). Even if OpenAI usually returns ordered results, this is a cheap hardening fix.
- The concurrency critique around live interjections looks real: rate state is checked, then model/post/write happens later without an obvious channel lock in [teams-live-recall-utterance.ts](C:/repos/oracle/apps/workers/src/trigger/teams-live-recall-utterance.ts:731) and related workers.
- The entity proposal SQL concern is real. The current `= ANY(SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text(...))` shape in [stage-entity-proposal.ts](C:/repos/oracle/packages/oracle-engines/src/extraction/stage-entity-proposal.ts:55) is suspicious enough that I’d test and likely rewrite it.

A few items are valid but I’d downgrade in urgency: taxonomy naming with regex JSON extraction, random k-means seeding, non-transactional synthesis follow-up writes, and retrieval domain keyword gaps. They matter, but they are backlog-quality unless you are actively relying on those admin workflows.

One nuance: the report slightly overstates/garbles the validation mechanics. The comment says `tryZodParse` throws on validation failure, but the code returns `null`; the assessment’s conclusion is still correct: failed validation is currently treated as usable output.

My priority order would be:

1. Fix chat prompt ordering and Anthropic cache eligibility.
2. Fix structured-output validation so invalid schema output throws a typed error or enters repair/fallback.
3. Pass `providerOptions` through OpenAI/DeepSeek `generateObject`.
4. Harden `embedMany`.
5. Add interjection concurrency protection.
6. Fix the entity-proposal SQL and add a DB-backed test.

So: good assessment. A little breathless in places, but not hand-wavy. The caching headline is the one I’d act on first.
